title: 第 18 節
en: Rv. II. 34, 13. ni-méghaminih ityena pigasi su-skandram varnam dadhire su-pésasam, ‘They (the Rudras) strongly showering down on their horse, made shining, beautiful light.’ (On pagas and its supposed connection with Peasox, see Kuhn, in his ‘ Zeitschrift,’ vol. i. p. 461; and Sonne, ibid. vol. x. p. 174 seq.)
Rv. IJ. 1, 12. tava spirhé varne, ‘In thy sparkling light, O Agni.’
Kv. III. 384, 5. pré imam varnam atirat sukram isim, ‘ He, Indra, spread out the bright light of the dawn.’
In the ninth Mandala the colour (varna) of the BELLEROPHON. 181 Soma juice is frequently mentioned, as h4ri, rusat, suki, also as asurya:
Rv. X. 3, 3. Agnih vi-tishthan ruisadbhih varnaih, ‘ Agni far-striding with shining colours.’

Even without determining adjectives, varna has occasionally the sense of light: Rv. I. 92, 10. samandm varnam abh{ sumbhamfné, ‘ The old Dawn that clothes herself in the same light.’ Rv. X. 124, 7. tah asya varnam sukayah bharibhrati, ‘ They (the dawns), the bright ones, carry always the light of the sun.’ See also Rv. II. 4, 5; If. 5,5; IV. 15, 3.

Hence we may take vara in the same sense in another passage, where the commentator explains it as Indra, the protector: | Rv. I. 104, 2. devalsah manyum dasasya skamnan té nah # vakshan suvitaya varnam, ‘The gods broke the pride of Dasa (the enemy); may they bring to us light for the sacrifice.’

Lastly, varna means colour, or tribe, or caste, the difference in colour being undoubtedly one of the principal causes of that feeling of strangeness ‘and heterogeneousness which found expression in the name of tribe, and, in India, of caste’. The commentators generally take varna in the technical sense of caste, and refer it to the three highest castes (traivarnika) in opposition to the fourth, the SAdras.
Rv. III. 34, 9. hatvf’ désyQn pra aryam varnam avah, ‘Indra, killing the Dasyus (the enemies), has protected the Aryan colour.’
Rv. I. 12. 4. yah d&sam varnam ddharam guha dkar, ‘Indra who brought the colour of the Dasas low in secret.’
Rv. IL. 3, 5. varnam punanéA yasdsam su-viram, ‘(The heavenly gates) which illuminate the glorious colour (race), rich in heroes.’
8 See my letter to Chevalier Bunsen, ‘On the Turanian Languages,’ p. 84.

But to return to varvara, to which on etymological grounds we should assign the meaning of shagey, hairy, villosus, it need hardly be said that such a word, though it supplies an intelligible meaning of the Greek mythe of Belleros, as slain by Bellerophon, does not occur in the Veda among the numerous names of the demons slain by Indra, Agni, and other bright gods. The same happens very frequently, viz. that Sanskrit supplies us with the etymological meaning of a term used in Greek mythology, although the corresponding word does not occur in the actual or mythological language of India. Thus the Greek Héré is easily explained by Svar&, or, according to Sonne (Kuhn, ‘ Zeitschrift,’ vol. x. p. 36°, vol. 1x. P- 202), by VasrA; but neither of these words occurs in the mythological phraseology of the Veda. There remains, however, a question which has still to be answered, viz. Do we find among the demons slain by solar deities, one to whom the name of varvara’, in the sense of shaggy, would be applicable? and this question we may answer with a decided Yes.

One of the principal enemies or dAsas conquered by Indra is the black cloud. This black cloud contains the rain or the fertilising waters which Indra is asked to send down upon the earth, and this he can only d do lo by slaying the black demon that keeps 9 BéAAepog may either be simply identified with varvara, in the sense of shaggy, or by taking FeAdog as representing the Latin villus, an adjective FeAAepog might have been formed, like ¢fove-po¢ from gHovoc. The transition into AA appears also in paddAoc, sheepswool, where the » represents the labial liquid. See Lobeck, ‘ De Prothesi et Apheresi,’ p. 111 seq.; and Curtius, in Kuhn’s ‘ Zeitschrift,’ vol. iii. p.410: papr=vrik; péeddwry=FérXewr; parny= vritha.
BELLEROPHON. 183 them in prison. This black cloud itself is sometimes spoken of in the Veda as the black skin:
Rv. IX. 41, 1. ghnantah krishniim apa tvékam, ‘Pushing away the black skin, i.e. cloud.’
In other places the cloud is called the rain-giving and fertilising skin: Rv. I. 129, 3. dasmah hi sma vrishanam pinvasi tvakam, ‘For thou, the strong one, fillest the rainy skin.’
While thus the cloud itself is spoken of as a black skin, the demon of the cloud, or the cloud personified, appears in the Veda as a ram, i.e. as a shaggy, hairy animal, in fact, as a Beaaspog.
Thus Urana, which, as we saw before, meant ram or laniger, is a name of a demon, slain by Indra:
Rv. II. 14. Ye priests, bring hither Soma for Indra, pour from the bowls the delicious food! The hero truly always loves to drink of it; sacrifice to the strong, for he desires it !
Ye priests, he who struck down Vritra, when he had hid the waters, as a tree is struck by lightning,—to him who desires this Soma, offer it; for that Indra desires to drink it!

Ye priests, he who slew Dribhika, who drove out the cows, for he had opened the stable, to him offer this Soma! Cover him with Somas as the wind in the sky, as an old woman covers herself with clothes !
Ye priests, he who slew Urana, who had shown his ninety-nine arms,—he who slew down to the ground Arbuda, that Indra call hither to the offering of Soma!

Here Urana is no doubt a proper name, but the idea which it suggested originally, could only have been that of urana, meaning ram or some other shagey animal. And the same applies to the Greek Béaascos. Though in Greek it has become a mere proper name, its original meaning was clearly that of the shaggy ram as the symbol of the shaggy. ' cloud, a monstrum villosum, this being the very 184 BELLEROPHON.
adjective which Roman poets like to apply to monsters of the same kith and kin, such as Gorgo or Cacus; e.g. Ov. ‘ Met.’ x. 21: Nec uti villosa colubris Terna Medusai vincirem guttura monstri.
‘ /En.’ vill. 266 (of Cacus): Terribiles oculos, vultum, villosaque setis Pectora semiferi . ...

We cannot therefore claim the name of Belleros or Bellerophon for that period of mythology which preceded the Aryan separation, a period during which such names as Dyaus=Zsvs, Varuna=Onpavoc, Ushas=’Heng, Saranyti=’Eziwis, Ahani=Aat¢vy and "ASrzvn, Ribhu=’Opdevg Haritas=Xapireg were current among the ancient worshippers of the Devas or bright gods. But we can see at least this, that Bellerophontes had an intelligible meaning, and a meaning analogous to that of other names of solar heroes, the enemies of the dark powers of nature, whether in the shape of night, or dark clouds, or winter. In the Veda one of the principal representatives of that class of demons is Vritra, literally the coverer, the hider, whether of light or rain. Indra, the great solar deity of the Veda, is emphatically called Vritrahan, the killer of Vritra. It is well known that the name of Indra, as the supreme deity of the Vedic pantheon, is a name of Indian growth. Derived from the same root as indu, drop, it represents the Jupiter pluvius, whose supremacy among the gods of India is fully accounted for by the climatic character of that country. Dyaus, i.e. Zeve, the god of the bright sky, the original supreme deity of the undivided Aryans, was replaced in India by Indra, who is sometimes called the son of Dyaus, so that in BELLEROPHON. 185 India the prophecy of Prometheus may be said to have been fulfilled, even before it was uttered under a Greek sky.

But though we must not look in Greek mythology for traces of a name like Indra, which did not spring into existence before the separation of the Aryans, it is not impossible that some of the names of Indra’s enemies may have been preserved in other countries. These enemies were the enemies of Dyaus and other gods as well as of Indra; and as they belong to an earlier period, the appearance of their names in the new homes of the Aryan emigrants could have nothing to surprise us.

One of the names belonging to this class of beings, hostile to men and the bright gods, and common to India and Greece, I observed many years ago, and having communicated my observation to several of iny friends, it was mentioned by them even before I found an opportunity of laying it before the public, and supporting it by sufficient proof. My excellent friend, Professor Trithen, whose early death has deprived Sanskrit scholarship of a man of real genius and high promise, mentioned my identification of Kerberos with the Sanskrit sarvara in a Paper read in April 1848, and published in the ‘ Transactions of the Philological Society ;’ and another learned friend of mine referred to it with approval a few years later, though neither of them represented correctly the steps by which I had arrived at my conclusion. My first point was that, as sdrvari in the Veda means the night, sarvara must have had the original sense of dark or pale:
Rv. V. 52, 3. té syandriiso né ukshanah ati skandanti sarvarth, ‘These (the Storm-gods), like powerful bulls, rise over the dark nights (or the dark clouds?).’
zh: 梨俱吠陀 II. 34, 13. ni-méghaminih ityena pigasi su-skandram varnam dadhire su-pésasam，「他們（魯陀羅）強烈地傾注於其馬上，造就了光輝燦爛、美麗的光芒。」（關於 pagas 及其與 Peasox 的推測關連，見 Kuhn 著《時間誌》第一卷第 461 頁；以及 Sonne，同上誌第十卷第 174 及以下各頁。）
《梨俱吠陀》第一卷第一首讚歌第12節。tava spirhé varne，「以汝熾烈之光，吾之火神啊。」
《梨俱吠陀》第三篇第384首第5節：pré imam varnam atirat sukram isim，「他，因陀羅，展開黎明的光芒。」
在第九部曲中，**貝勒羅豐**的顏色（varna）經常被提及為《蘇摩》汁液，如 h4ri、rusat、suki，亦稱為 asurya：
梨俱吠陀第十卷第三讚歌第三節。Agnih vi-tishthan ruisadbhih varnaih，「阿格尼遠步而行，以耀眼之色彩而閃耀。」

即使沒有限定形容詞，*varna* 也偶爾具有光的含義：《梨俱吠陀》I. 92, 10. *samandm varnam abh{ sumbhamfné*，「穿著同樣光芒的古老晨曦」。《梨俱吠陀》X. 124, 7. *tah asya varnam sukayah bharibhrati*，「它們（晨曦），那些光亮者，總是承載著太陽的光」。亦見《梨俱吠陀》II. 4, 5；5, 5；IV. 15, 3。
因此，在另一段經文中，我們可以用相同的意義理解 vara，該處注疏者將其解釋為因陀羅（保護者）：| 梨俱吠陀 I. 104, 2. devalsah manyum dasasya skamnan té nah # vakshan suvitaya varnam，「諸神摧毀了大薩人（敵人）的驕傲；願他們為我們帶來祭祀的光明。」
最後，*varna* 意指顏色、部族或種姓，膚色的差異無疑是產生那種陌生感與異質性感受的主要原因之一，這種感受在部族的名稱中，以及在印度的種姓制度中，得到了表達。評論家通常在技術意義上取 *varna* 為種姓，並將其指涉於三個最高的種姓（*traivarnika*），與第四個種姓首陀羅（*Sūdras*）相對立。
梨俱吠陀第三卷第三十四讚歌第九節：hatvā́ dásyūn pra aryam varnam avah，「因陀羅殺戮達斯尤人（敵人），保護了雅利安人的膚色。」
梨俱吠陀 I. 12. 4. yah d&sam varnam ddharam guha dkar，「因陀羅在隱秘處壓低了達薩人的膚色。」
梨俱吠陀第二卷第3讚，第5詩節。varnam punanéA yasdsam su-viram，『（諸天門）照亮榮耀之色（種族），英雄繁盛。』
見我致布森騎士（Chevalier Bunsen）的信函，〈論圖蘭語族〉，第84頁。

但回到 varvara 這個詞，我們應當以詞源學的理由將其義界定為「毛茸茸的、多毛的、villous」（villous），幾乎無須贅言，儘管這樣的詞彙為被貝勒洛豐（Bellerophon）所殺的希臘神話中的貝勒洛斯（Belleros）提供了一個可理解的含義，但在吠陀中卻不見於因陀羅（Indra）、阿耆尼（Agni）及其他光明之神所殺的眾多魔鬼名稱之中。這種情況極其頻繁地出現，即梵文為我們供應了一個在希臘神話中使用的詞彙的詞源學含義，儘管相應的詞彙並未出現在印度實際的或神話的語言中。因此，希臘文的 Héré 可輕易地由 Svar&，或按照松內（Sonne）（庫恩《時代誌》第十卷第 36° 頁、第 ix 卷第 202 頁）的意見，由 VasrA 來解釋；但這兩個詞彙都不出現在吠陀的神話用語中。然而，仍有一個問題有待解答，即：我們是否在太陽神所殺的魔鬼之中發現某一個，其名稱 varvara 在「毛茸茸的」之義上會適用於他？而這個問題我們可以明確地回答為「是」。

印陀羅所征服的主要敵人或提婆（dAsas）之一是黑雲。這片黑雲蘊含著雨水或使大地肥沃的水，人們祈求印陀羅將之降下人間，而他唯有擊殺那個禁錮此黑色惡魔的方式才能辦到。βέλλερος（bélleros）既可簡單地與 varvara（毛髮蓬亂之意）相同，亦可取 βέλλος（béllós）代表拉丁文 villus，由此一形容詞 βέλλερος（bélleros）可能已形成，正如 φίονε-ρος（phióne-ros）源自 φίονος（phíonos）。這種向 αα 的轉變亦見於 παδδλος（paddlos，羊毛之意），其中的 ρ 代表脣音流音。見洛貝克（Lobeck）著《論前綴與脫落》（De Prothesi et Apheresi），第 111 頁及以下；及柯蒂烏斯（Curtius）在庫恩主編《時代誌》（Zeitschrift）第三卷第 410 頁：paρ=vrik；ρέδδωρυ=FέρXewr；parny= vritha。
貝勒羅豐。183 將他們監禁。這片黑雲本身有時在《吠陀》中被稱為黑色皮膚：
梨俱吠陀第九卷第41讚第1節。ghnantah krishniim apa tvékam，「推開黑色的皮膚，即雲彩。」
在其他地方，雲被稱為賦予雨水與施予肥沃的皮膚：《梨俱吠陀》I. 129, 3. dasmah hi sma vrishanam pinvasi tvakam，「蓋汝強者充盈降雨之皮膚。」
當雲本身被說成是黑色的皮膚時，雲的惡魔，或被擬人化的雲，在《吠陀》中顯現為一隻公羊，即一種毛茸茸、長毛的動物，實際上，乃是一個 Beaaspog。
因此，烏拉那（Urana）——正如我們先前所見，其原義為公羊或長毛獸——乃是一位被因陀羅所誅滅的魔神之名：
梨俱吠陀 II. 14. 祭司們，為因陀羅獻上蘇摩吧，從器皿中倒出這美味的食物！英雄確實總是樂於飲用它；獻祭於強大者吧，因為他渴望它！
諸位祭司啊，當因陀羅如樹為閃電所擊，擊落隱匿眾水之弗栗多時——將此蘇摩酒獻予欲飲之人；因為因陀羅渴望飲此酒！
諸位祭司，對於那殺死德里布希卡（Dribhika）、趕走牲畜者，因為他曾打開牲畜圈，獻上蘇摩祭品給他！如同天空中的風、如同老婦人用衣裳蔽身一般，用蘇摩祭品覆蓋他！
諸位祭司們，他殺死了烏剌那（Urana），那展現九十九臂者；他將阿爾布達（Arbuda）擊倒至地，願那因陀羅（Indra）來臨於蘇摩（Soma）的祭祀！
這裡，烏剌那（Urana）無疑是一個專有名詞，但它最初所暗示的概念，只能是烏剌那（urana）的意義，即公羊或其他蓬毛動物。同樣的情況也適用於希臘文的貝萊洛豐（Béaascos）。儘管在希臘文中它已成為單純的專有名詞，其原始意義顯然是蓬毛的公羊，象徵著蓬毛的雲，即所謂「毛茸茸的怪物」（monstrum villosum），這正是其本質所在。
形容詞，為羅馬詩人喜歡用來形容同類怪物的用語，如戈爾貢（Gorgo）或卡庫斯（Cacus）之類；例如見於《變形記》第十卷第二十一行：「我不會像對付長毛的三頭美杜莎怪物那樣束縛住她的喉嚨」（Nec uti villosa colubris Terna Medusai vincirem guttura monstri）。
/En.' viii. 266（關於卡庫斯）：Terribiles oculos, vultum, villosaque setis / Pectora semiferi ...

因此，我們無法將貝勒羅斯（Belleros）或貝勒洛豐（Bellerophon）這樣的名字追溯至雅利安分化之前的神話時期，在那個時期，諸如特尤斯=宙斯（Dyaus=Zeus）、伐樓那=歐拉諾斯（Varuna=Ouranos）、烏莎絲=厄俄斯（Ushas=Heos）、莎蘭雨底=塞瑞妮斯（Saranyti=Hserinis）、阿哈尼=阿忒透美斯（Ahani=Artemis）和「阿耶尼」（Asravn），以及利布（Ribhu）=歐瑞翁（Orpheus）、哈利塔斯=卡瑞斯（Haritas=Charites）等名字在古代提婆或光明之神的崇拜者之間流傳。但我們至少可以看出，貝勒洛豐特斯（Bellerophontes）具有可以理解的含義，且其含義與其他太陽英雄名字類似，這些英雄乃自然界黑暗力量的敵人，無論該黑暗力量以夜晚、烏雲或冬季的形式出現。在《吠陀》中，該類惡魔的主要代表之一是毗栗多羅（Vritra），字面意思為「遮蔽者」、「隱藏者」，無論是隱藏光線還是隱藏雨水。吠陀中偉大的太陽神因陀羅（Indra）被明確地稱為毗栗多羅殺者（Vritrahan，毗栗多羅的殺者）。眾所周知，因陀羅作為吠陀神殿中最高神祇的名字是印度本土發展而來的。該名字源自與因度（indu，水滴）相同的字根，代表木星普路維烏斯（Jupiter pluvius），其在印度眾神中的至高地位完全可由該國的氣候特徵來說明。特尤斯（Dyaus），即宙斯（Zeus），明亮天空之神、未分化雅利安人原始至高神，在印度被因陀羅取代，因陀羅有時被稱為特尤斯之子，因此在印度，普羅米修斯的預言可以說已經應驗，遠早於它在希臘天空下被提出之前。
然而，儘管我們不應在希臘神話中尋找印陀羅（Indra）這樣的名稱痕跡——因為此名在雅利安人分離之前尚未出現——但印陀羅的敵人的某些名稱有可能在其他國家得以保留下來，這並非不可能。這些敵人既是特尤斯及其他諸神的敵人，亦是印陀羅的敵人；且由於他們屬於一個更早的時期，這些名稱在雅利安移民者的新家園中出現，實在不足為奇。

我在多年前觀察到一個屬於此類存在的名字，這類存在對人類與光明諸神懷有敵意，且在印度與希臘之間通用。在我找到機會將其呈諸公眾並用充分證據支持之前，我已將此觀察傳達給數位友人，他們甚至早於我發表之時就已提及。我尊敬的朋友特里森（Trithen）教授——其英年早逝使梵文學問喪失了一位真正天才與前景光明的學者——曾在一八四八年四月宣讀的論文中提及我將刻耳柏洛斯（Kerberos）與梵文的sarvara相認同，該論文刊登於《語文學會交易集》（Transactions of the Philological Society）；另一位博學的友人在數年後也附議提及此說，儘管他們皆未準確呈現我推導結論的步驟。我的第一個論點是：既然吠陀中的sarvari意為夜晚，那麼sarvara必定原有深色或蒼白之義：
梨俱吠陀卷五讚歌五十二第三節：té syandriiso né ukshanah ati skandanti sarvarth，「彼等（風暴諸神），如同強大之公牛，騰升於黑暗之夜間（或黑暗之雲？）。」
